title: Restapling a chair seat after re-foaming
subtitle: Refit the top cover on a drop-in chair seat with fresh foam underneath
excerpt: How to staple a new top cover onto a drop-in dining chair seat after fresh foam has gone in. Covers tensioning the fabric, stapling order, corner folds, and finishing the back face with calico.
sourceNotes: Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual. Joyce Storey Upholstery practical handbooks.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dining or hall chair seat built as a separate frame that drops into a rebate in the main chair frame. Lifts out for re-upholstery without disturbing the chair joints.
The decorative outer fabric of an upholstered seat. Sits over the foam and any calico underlayer. Stapled to the underside of the seat frame at the perimeter.

content.text: The
top cover
stage on a
drop-in seat
is the last step of the re-upholster. Fresh foam is already cut and bonded; new calico is stretched and stapled. The top cover goes on next.
Step 1, cut the fabric to size

Lay the fabric face down on a clean table. Place the seat frame face down on the fabric, centred. Cut around the frame with a 75 mm margin on all sides. Check the pattern alignment if the fabric has a print or stripe. The print should run square to the front rail.
Step 2, staple the front rail first

Pull the fabric over the front edge of the seat and tension lightly with one hand. Drive one staple in the middle of the front rail to hold the fabric. Work out to each corner with three more staples spaced 50 mm apart. Keep the fabric tension even; uneven tension shows as ridges across the seat once the cover is on.
Step 3, staple the back rail

Pull the fabric to the back and tension across the foam. The fabric should sit smooth across the top of the seat without lumps. Drive one staple in the middle of the back rail, then work out to each corner with three more, the same spacing as the front.
Step 4, staple the side rails

Tension the fabric from one side. One staple in the middle, then work out toward the corners with three more. Repeat on the opposite side. Check the fabric across the top of the seat at every step. A loose spot shows as a sag; pull the fabric tighter on the affected side and add a staple to hold the new tension.
Step 5, fold the corners

At each corner, pull the loose fabric straight off the corner. Fold the fabric on the diagonal into a triangle and tuck the edge in flat against the side rail. Hold the fold and drive two staples through it into the rail. Trim any excess fabric with the upholstery shears. The corner should sit flat, not stand proud.
Step 6, trim and finish the back face

Trim the loose fabric off all four rails with the shears, leaving a 10 mm flap stapled down. Cut a piece of black hessian or calico to fit the underside of the seat. Fold the edges under and staple in place to cover the raw fabric edges. This dust cover is the back face that shows when the seat is lifted out of the chair.
Step 7, drop the seat back into the chair

Place the seat into the chair rebate from above. The seat should drop in flush. If it sits proud, the foam is too thick and needs trimming; if it sits low, the foam has compressed under the cover and needs a calico shim. A small pressure with the palm should not move the seat in any direction.
Variations

On a sprung dining-chair seat, the stapling order is the same but a calico underlayer is essential between the springs and the top cover. On a leather seat, the corner folds are cut and bonded with leather adhesive rather than folded; leather does not fold cleanly into a flat triangle.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Diagonal ridges across the top of the seat
attrs.cause: Uneven fabric tension at the rails. One side was pulled tighter than the other.
attrs.fix: Pull the staples on the loose side with the back of the claw hammer. Re-tension the fabric and re-staple. Work from the middle out to each corner.

attrs.symptom: The fabric pattern is off-square once finished
attrs.cause: The fabric was not aligned to the front rail before stapling, or the front rail was not square to the back rail.
attrs.fix: Lift the staples on three sides, leaving only the front rail stapled. Re-align the fabric and re-staple the other three rails.

attrs.symptom: Staples pull out of the rail under tension
attrs.cause: Soft or rotten rail timber, or staples too short for the frame thickness.
attrs.fix: Switch to longer staples (12 mm or 14 mm). If the rail is soft, glue a hardwood strip along the rail edge as a fixing strip before re-stapling.
